P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95812

FPPC

AUG 13 1992
[illegible]

Re: Las Tunas Beach Geologic Hazard
    Abatement District
    Your File No. A-92-328

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     Thank you for your letter of August 6, 1992 providing
advice on behalf of Members of the Las Tunas Beach Geologic
Hazard Abatement District Board of Directors. The purpose of
this letter is to request that your advice letter be supplemented
giving consideration to the property ownerships and
boardmembership of Geary Steffen and Donald Briscoe.

     At the time of my request for advice letter dated May
27, 1992, it was understood that original Boardmember Donald
Briscoe had resigned and that Arnold Bernstein had replaced him.
It appears that that resignation was never completed, however,
and it has since been withdrawn. Accordingly, Donald Briscoe is
a current Member of the Board of Directors.

     With respect to Chairman of the Board of Directors,
Geary Steffen, the Board of Directors has been informed by Mr.
Steffen that he owns an interest in the property at 19028 Pacific
Coast Highway of which his wife, Mary L. Morgan/Steffen is the
record titleholder. The Board of Directors have been further
informed that Mr. Steffen's interest in that property has existed

26379.1

Mr. John W. Wallace
August 11, 1992
Page 2




since prior to the formation of the Geologic Hazard Abatement
District.

     The information submitted to you on July 27, 1992
containing a listing of property ownerships and a map which
identified the location of all Boardmembers' properties does show
the information you may need to give consideration to the
ownerships and locations of properties of Boardmembers Geary
Steffen and Donald Briscoe in relation to the groins.

     Would you please supplement you opinion of August 6,
1992 by providing advice with respect to these two additional
Boardmembers.

     If you need any further information relative to this
request, please don't hesitate to contact me.


                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        CARL K. NEWTON
                                        for LAS TUNAS BEACH GEOLOGIC
                                        HAZARD ABATEMENT DISTRICT; and
                                        of BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN

Mr. John Wallace
Staff Attorney
FAIR POLICIAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Las Tunas Beach Geologic Hazard
Abatement District

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Pursuant to our telephone conversation of this date, I
am submitting this letter as General Counsel for the Las Tunas
Beach Geologic Hazard Abatement District (GHAD) to provide you
with supplemental data which may be of assistance in providing to
the GHAD a formal advice letter. In our recent telephone
conversations, you indicated that you needed information as to
the total population of the District and the proximity of
properties owned by Board Members to the groins.

Enclosed is a map of the GHAD showing all properties
within the District, together with the location of the groins and
the location of the properties owned by the five members of the
Board of directors. I am informed that there are eighty single
family dwellings within the District, three multiple dwellings,
and five vacant lots, two of which are under construction of
single family dwellings. The three multiple dwellings consist of
two having six units each and one having four units.

24740.1

Mr. John Wallace
July 27, 1992
Page 2


I have been informed by engineering consultants that
the correct location of the groins is indicated by the State
Lands Commission Survey of 1960. Please refer to the two legends
appearing on the maps, which have underlined the markings for
that SLC Survey.

Also enclosed is a list of the property ownerships
within the District by address on Topanga Beach Road and Pacific
Coast Highway. The five Board Members and their properties have
been underlined.

If you should need any further information in
connection with this matter, please do not hesitate to contact
me.

Sincerely,

CARL K. NEWTON
of BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL
(213) 236-2708

OUR FILE NO. 02669-001

May 27, 1992

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re: Requested Formal Advice Letter

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

I serve as the general counsel for the Las Tunas Beach
Geologic Hazard Abatement District (GHAD). The five-member board
of the GHAD has directed that I request a formal advice letter
from the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding whether a
conflict of interest would exist if the board members voted on
measures for the GHAD which will affect their property. The key
question in this case is if the GHAD's decision will affect a
boardmember's interest in substantially the same manner as it
will affect all members of the public or a significant segment of
the public. The question is based on the following facts:

The GHAD was formed, pursuant to a resolution of the Malibu
City Council in 1991, to determine what should be done with eight
groins which were built within the area of the GHAD. A groin is
a type of steel sea wall built from the beach into the ocean
which is used to prevent beach erosion. The GHAD will also
consider secondary issues including beach erosion if the groins

LS733-2

JUN 7 1992

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
May 27, 1992
Page 2


are removed and not replaced and the possibility of adding sand
to Las Tunas Beach.

     The area in the GHAD consists of 81 parcels along Las Tunas
Beach.  Under Public Resources Code § 26567, since the Malibu
City Council did not appoint itself to the GHAD board, the board
must consist of real property owners within the ecologic hazard
abatement district.  Four of the GHAD boardmembers--Robert
MacLeod, Arnold Bernstein, Fay Singer and Cindy Parks--own or co-
own parcels of land on which one of the groins is actually
located or within 90 to 100 feet of the boardmembers' property.
Boardmember Geary Steffen's wife owns a parcel that is within 90
feet of one of the groins.  All of the parcels in question have a
fair market value of more than $1,000.

     As stated above, as part of its mandate, the GHAD board will
be determining exacting what should be done with the groins.
Because of the proximity of the groins to each of the
boardmember's property, the GHAD's decisions will specially
benefit or affect the boardmembers' property.  In fact, under
state law, all land within a GHAD must be specially benefitted by
any construction proposed by the GHAD.  (Public Resources Code §§
26534, 26571.)

     A review of the language in several FPPC opinions, however
appears to provide authority for the proposition that the GHAD
board members' interest is the same as all members of the public
or a significant segment thereof and therefore not a conflict of
interest.  (Government Code section 87103; FPPC Section 18703.)
In 2 FPPC Opinion 77, the FPPC considered whether a planning
commissioner was prohibited from making, participating in, or in
any way using their official position to influence the adoption
of a specific land use plan for a city's core area when the
commissioner owned a home across the street from one boundary of
the core area in a neighborhood which was viewed as being
inextricably tied to the core area in terms of planning
considerations.  The core area included both the downtown
shopping area and a number of residential dwellings.

     In reaching its decision, the FPPC stated:

     "[W]e conclude that residential home owners within and
     in the immediate vicinity of the "core area" constitute
     a "significant segment" of the public and Planning
     Commissioner Hunt accordingly need not disqualify
     himself from participating merely because he owns a

15733.2

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
May 27, 1992
Page 3


home as described.  While certain aspects of the plan
may operate to increase the value of this home, either
as a residential or commercial property, it is apparent
that the plan will have a "substantially similar"
effect on numerous other residential properties,
perhaps throughout the entire city as well as those
near the "core area."

(Id. at 81.)

In 4 FPPC Opinions 13 (1978), the FPPC stated:

"As is noted in our analysis of the facts before us, we
have previously interpreted the "public generally" rule
of [Government Code] Section 87103 in a manner which
requires disqualification unless the decision will
affect the interests of all or a significant segment of
the public within the decision-maker's jurisdiction in
substantially the same manner.  This is not a per se
rule but rather one which requires examination of each
situation to determine if a particular public
official's financial interests is distinguishable from
the effect on the public generally."

(Id. at 25 (emphasis added).)

We look forward to your response to this question.  If you
need any additional information regarding this matter, please
contact either myself, Rufus Young, Esq. or Jeff Kightlinger,
Esq.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        CARL K. NEWTON
                                        of BURKE, WILLIAMS & SORENSEN
